## Recommendations for Practitioners [PLACEHOLDER]
<!-- [PLACEHOLDER] -->

The following recommendations are directed at practitioners who wish to implement
deep RL trading systems in settings similar to the one studied
here. They are grounded in the specific lessons above rather than
general RL best practices.

**Invest in feature engineering before algorithm selection.** The results
demonstrate that feature quality is the primary driver of out-of-sample policy
performance in this setting. Before experimenting with alternative algorithms or
architectural variants, practitioners should verify that the state representation
adequately captures the informational structure of the order book: order flow
imbalance, multi-level spread and depth features, book shape measures, and
temporal context. A thorough feature validation — examining the correlation
structure, stationarity, and predictive content of each feature on held-out data
— is more valuable time spent than hyperparameter search over a fixed but
impoverished state space.

**Enforce a strict chronological evaluation protocol from the start.** Any form
of lookahead — features computed from future data, normalization parameters
estimated on the full dataset, or train/test splits that are not strictly
sequential — will produce a materially optimistic assessment of out-of-sample
performance. In high-frequency settings, where the information decay of market
signals is measured in seconds, even mild lookahead can generate the appearance
of a viable strategy from what is effectively a leakage artifact. Normalization
should be fit on the training split only and applied frozen to validation and
test data. This is not just good practice; it is the minimum requirement for the
empirical results to be meaningful.

**Treat transaction cost sensitivity as a go/no-go criterion.** The threshold at
which the agent's apparent edge disappears under transaction costs is a property
of the strategy that must be known before drawing conclusions. Practitioners
should run the same evaluation across a range of cost levels — from zero to the
realistic spread-plus-fee regime of their target venue — and identify whether
the strategy's risk-adjusted return degrades gracefully or collapses abruptly.
An edge that survives only at zero cost is not a tradeable strategy; an edge
that degrades proportionally with costs at least establishes the direction of
improvement required.

**Keep reward optimization and financial evaluation strictly separate.** The
Differential Sharpe Ratio or any other shaped reward is a training objective,
not a performance report. All financial conclusions — Sharpe ratio, annualized
return, maximum drawdown — should be derived from a deterministic policy rollout
on held-out data, computed from the realized log-return series. Results from the
training reward signal should appear in a dedicated section as training diagnostics,
clearly labeled as such, and should not be cited as evidence of financial viability.
This discipline is the single most consistent failure mode in the published
literature and the most straightforward to avoid.
